text: This quick edit tip is all about drones. What's up, man? They're working you good out here, huh? Nothing I don't want to be doing. Sometimes when you're editing, you'll find that the footage doesn't necessarily need a full score of melodic music to serve the story and the emotion. That just might be too much. Sometimes it would be better served with a more understated ambience, which you can usually create with a combination of tones and drones. Let's begin with the basic definition of a drone. A drone is a sustained sound that can be pitched higher or lower. It often establishes a tonality upon which the rest of the piece is built. A drone can come from a real instrument, a vocal sound, or from an electronic keyboard such as a synthesizer or a sampler Let's look at some common types of drones that you might use in a cut. We've chosen three samples to cover a wide spectrum of moods and emotions. First is a sound you're probably very familiar with, the low ominous drone. This drone is created digitally using synths and pads. It's a staple tool in the editor's arsenal for creating danger and tension wherever those feelings are called for. It's pretty hard to go wrong with drones like this unless, of course, you overuse them. Our second common type of drone is light and peaceful. Have a listen. You can imagine using a drone like this whenever you need to create a hopeful, dreamlike, or ethereal mood. Now our third type of drone exists somewhere in the middle. It can usher in pretty much any type of emotion or gently blend with any kind of music, making it much easier to transition in and out of sections of a cut. Have a listen. This drone happens to have been created digitally with synths as well, though it does have the warm feeling of sustained string instruments. It is, for the most part, pretty light and warm, though it does introduce some tension. The nice thing about these "middle of the road sounding" drones is that they're flexible. You can use them on their own or for leading into a wide variety of music. The drone might begin and carry on for as long as you need it to before you gently ease into full score like this. That works great, right? A drone like this is the perfect tool to gradually bring on some music after a dry scene without the music feeling like it comes out of nowhere. It's interesting to note here that when played by itself, the drone has a relatively ambiguous feeling. And thus, it's able to transition into an cue, with this particular cue being relatively upbeat and cheerful. But it could just as easily transition us into something scary and dark.
